@graph.headline: Why Your Construction Profit and Cash Disagree: Reading a WIP Schedule
@graph.description: Why construction P&L profit and bank cash diverge: over billing as a liability, under billing as an early sign of profit fade, and retainage. How to read a WIP schedule line by line with a worked job, the monthly balanced journal entries, and the cash flow discipline that closes the gap.
@graph.keywords: construction WIP schedule, work in progress report, over billing, under billing, billings in excess of costs, costs in excess of billings, percentage of completion, profit fade, retainage receivable, construction cash flow, WIP journal entries, QuickBooks Online construction accounting, FinBoard.ai

mainEntity.name: What does it mean when a job is over billed?

acceptedAnswer.text: Over billing means you have invoiced more than the revenue you have earned based on percent complete. The excess sits on the balance sheet as a liability called billings in excess of costs and estimated earnings. The cash is real, but the work it pays for has not happened yet, so treat the balance as a job's advance, not as profit or free operating cash.

mainEntity.name: Is under billing always a bad sign?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. Under billing is normal when costs land just before a billing cycle closes, or when an approved change order has not been invoiced yet. It becomes a warning when the same job stays under billed for two or three months running, or when the balance grows while the billing excuse stays the same. Persistent under billing is often cost overrun that nobody has pushed into the estimate yet, which is how profit fade starts.

mainEntity.name: How do I calculate percent complete on a WIP schedule?

acceptedAnswer.text: Divide cost incurred to date by the current estimated total cost of the job. If you have spent 624,000 dollars against an estimated total cost of 960,000 dollars, the job is 65 percent complete. Multiply that percentage by the contract value to get earned revenue. The method only works if the estimated total cost is updated every month; a stale estimate makes every downstream number wrong.

mainEntity.name: What is profit fade and how do I catch it early?

acceptedAnswer.text: Profit fade is the pattern where a job's estimated gross margin shrinks as the job progresses, so profit recognized in early months gets clawed back near completion. Catch it by tracking each job's estimated margin month over month on the WIP schedule, questioning any drop from the bid margin, and treating aging under billings as unrecognized cost overruns until someone proves otherwise.

content:
Why Your Construction Profit and Cash Disagree: Reading a WIP Schedule

Every contractor hits this conversation eventually. The P&L for the quarter shows 300,000 dollars of profit, and the bank account can barely cover next week's payroll. Or the opposite, which is sneakier: cash is comfortable, the crews are busy, and then the year end WIP adjustment wipes out most of the profit you thought you had. Neither version means someone is stealing or that your bookkeeper is bad at math. It means profit and cash in construction are measured on different clocks, and the only report that reconciles the two clocks is the work in progress schedule.

This article is for contractors and the controllers who close their books. We'll walk through why the two numbers diverge, how to read a WIP schedule line by line with a worked job, the month end journal entries that keep the general ledger honest, and the discipline that keeps a timing difference from quietly becoming a real loss.

The Three Timing Wedges Between Profit and Cash

On a percentage of completion job, revenue is earned as cost is incurred, but cash arrives on whatever schedule your progress billings and payment terms produce. Three wedges open up between the two, and each one lives on the balance sheet.

Over billing: cash ahead of earnings

When you invoice faster than you earn, you're over billed. The formal balance sheet line is billings in excess of costs and estimated earnings, and it is a liability. That word surprises people, because over billing feels great. Cash comes in early, the account looks healthy, and nobody complains. But the money you collected pays for work you haven't performed yet. The labor, material, and subcontractor cost to finish that work is still coming, and the cash to cover it is already in your account, quietly getting spent on other things. An over billed contractor who treats the balance as profit is borrowing from his own jobs. It works right up until the jobs finish and the borrowing has to be repaid in the form of costs with no matching billings left.

Under billing: earnings ahead of cash

The mirror image. You've incurred cost and earned revenue, but the invoices haven't gone out or haven't caught up. The balance sheet line is costs and estimated earnings in excess of billings, and it is an asset. Under billing feels poor. The P&L says you made money and the bank account disagrees. Sometimes the reason is innocent: costs landed two days before the billing cycle closed, or an approved change order hasn't been added to the schedule of values yet. But under billing is also, very often, the first smell of profit fade. If costs are running over estimate and nobody has updated the estimate, the job looks further along than it really is, earned revenue is overstated, and the under billing balance is really an unrecognized loss wearing an asset's clothes. Every under billed job deserves a direct question: is this a billing timing issue, or is this a cost problem we haven't admitted yet?

Retainage: earned but unbillable

The third wedge is contractual. Most commercial contracts let the owner hold back 5 to 10 percent of every progress billing until substantial completion or some milestone. That retainage is revenue you have earned and billed, and cash you cannot touch, sometimes for months after the job closes out. A contractor running 10 percent retainage on 8 million dollars of annual volume has 800,000 dollars of earned cash locked up at any given time. Retainage doesn't distort profit, but it absolutely distorts cash, and it belongs on the WIP schedule and the balance sheet as its own receivable so nobody mistakes it for collectible current AR.

How to Read a WIP Schedule Line by Line

A WIP schedule is one row per open job, and every column answers a specific question. Let's walk one worked job, then look at a small schedule.

Take a warehouse shell job. The contract value, including approved change orders, is 1,200,000 dollars. The current estimated total cost is 960,000 dollars, which means the job carries an estimated gross profit of 240,000 dollars, a 20 percent margin. Cost incurred to date is 624,000 dollars.

  • Percent complete = cost to date divided by estimated total cost. 624,000 / 960,000 = 65 percent. Cost is the input that drives everything, which is why a stale cost estimate poisons the whole row.

  • Earned revenue = percent complete times contract value. 65 percent of 1,200,000 = 780,000 dollars. This is the revenue that belongs on the P&L to date, no matter what has been invoiced.

  • Earned gross profit = earned revenue minus cost to date. 780,000 minus 624,000 = 156,000 dollars, which is exactly 65 percent of the 240,000 estimated profit. The math self checks, and you should run that check on every row.

  • Billed to date comes from the invoice register: 845,000 dollars on this job.

  • Over or under billing = billed to date minus earned revenue. 845,000 minus 780,000 = 65,000 dollars over billed. That 65,000 is a liability, not a bonus.

Now put that job next to a second one, a tenant improvement running at a thinner 15 percent margin, and read the schedule the way a surety or a bank would.

JobContractEst. costCost to date% completeEarned revenueBilled to dateOver / (under)
Warehouse shell1,200,000960,000624,00065%780,000845,00065,000
Tenant improvement800,000680,000510,00075%600,000540,000(60,000)
Total2,000,0001,640,0001,134,0001,380,0001,385,0005,000

Check the tenant improvement row yourself: 510,000 / 680,000 = 75 percent complete, 75 percent of 800,000 = 600,000 earned, billed only 540,000, so the job is 60,000 under billed. At the company level the two jobs nearly cancel, netting to 5,000 over billed. That net number is what makes WIP schedules dangerous to skim. A lender glancing at the total sees almost nothing. A controller reading line by line sees one job that has collected 65,000 dollars of someone else's future work, and another job where 60,000 dollars of earned profit hasn't turned into an invoice, and needs to know why on both counts.

The Monthly WIP Journal Entries

The WIP schedule only matters if it lands in the general ledger, and the mechanics are simpler than most people expect. Each month, reverse the prior month's over and under billing entries, recompute the schedule, and book the new balances. Debits equal credits on every entry, always.

For the warehouse job, billings of 845,000 exceed earned revenue of 780,000, so revenue on the books must come down by the over billed amount:

  • Debit: Contract revenue 65,000

  • Credit: Billings in excess of costs and estimated earnings (liability) 65,000

For the tenant improvement, earned revenue of 600,000 exceeds billings of 540,000, so the books must pick up revenue the invoices haven't caught yet:

  • Debit: Costs and estimated earnings in excess of billings (asset) 60,000

  • Credit: Contract revenue 60,000

Retainage gets its own reclass so current AR stays honest. If the warehouse contract holds 10 percent, then 84,500 of the 845,000 billed is not collectible until completion:

  • Debit: Retainage receivable 84,500

  • Credit: Accounts receivable 84,500

Three entries, each balanced, and now the P&L shows earned revenue rather than billed revenue, the balance sheet shows the over billing as the liability it is, and AR aging stops pretending retainage will arrive in 30 days. Book these every month, not just at year end when the CPA forces the issue. A contractor who only adjusts WIP annually spends eleven months making decisions on a P&L that is simply wrong.

Profit Fade: When Under Billing Never Converts

Here is the failure mode that ends contracting companies. Percent complete runs on estimated total cost. If field costs are overrunning and the project manager keeps reporting the original estimate, cost to date divided by a too small denominator says the job is further along than it is. Earned revenue gets overstated, early months show healthy margin, and the schedule shows a growing under billing that everyone explains as a billing timing issue. Then the job approaches completion, reality forces the estimate up, percent complete snaps backward, and revenue already recognized has to be given back. The margin you booked in March evaporates in November. That is profit fade, and on a fixed price job it can turn a 15 percent bid into a 4 percent close, or a loss.

The defense is a short list of questions asked about every job, every month:

  • Has the estimated total cost been updated this month, by someone who has seen the field, not just the original budget?

  • What is the job's current estimated margin versus its bid margin, and who can explain any drop?

  • Is this under billing new, or is it the same balance aging for a second or third month?

  • Are unapproved change orders sitting in earned revenue? Work performed on a verbal promise is cost with no contract value behind it until the paperwork exists.

  • Does cost to date include committed subcontractor and material costs we know about but haven't received invoices for?

Margin trend by job, month over month, is the single most predictive report in the industry. Fade almost never announces itself in one month. It leaks, two points here, three points there, and the WIP schedule is the only place the leak is visible early enough to fix pricing, staffing, or the estimate itself.

Cash Flow Discipline: Managing the Wedges Instead of Suffering Them

Once you can read the schedule, you can manage it. Three habits separate contractors who control their cash from contractors who are surprised by it.

Front load billings, ethically. There is nothing wrong with negotiating a schedule of values that weights mobilization, engineering, and early phases fairly, or with billing deposits your contract allows. Being modestly over billed on purpose, with eyes open, is healthy working capital management. The line you don't cross is billing for phantom progress, and the internal rule that keeps you honest is simple: know your over billing per job, and never let the total fund fixed overhead. Over billing is a job's money on loan to you. Spend it on that job's costs.

Bill retainage the moment it's billable. Retainage has a habit of outliving the job by a year because nobody owns the final paperwork. Track release milestones on the WIP schedule, invoice the day substantial completion or the contractual trigger hits, and chase punch list items like the receivable they are blocking. On thin margin work, the retainage often is the profit.

Chase the over and under monthly. Review every job's over or under position at month end with the PM in the room. Over billed jobs get a cost to complete check, since the remaining work is already paid for. Under billed jobs get the fade questions above, plus a billing action item with a date on it. Thirty minutes per month per job, and the year end WIP surprise stops existing.

Contractors who run multiple entities, one company per license, per state, or per joint venture, have the extra problem of reading WIP and cash across several QuickBooks files at once. If that's you, we've compared every method for combining reports from multiple QuickBooks Online companies. And if you're a firm controller keeping WIP discipline across a book of contractor clients, the playbook in how accounting firms deliver consolidated reports across 50 QuickBooks clients covers how to standardize this monthly routine at scale.

Take it with you: the free Construction WIP Schedule template computes every column in this article across your jobs, with over and under billing flagged.

FAQ

What does it mean when a job is over billed?

Over billing means you have invoiced more than the revenue you have earned based on percent complete. The excess sits on the balance sheet as a liability called billings in excess of costs and estimated earnings. The cash is real, but the work it pays for has not happened yet, so treat the balance as a job's advance, not as profit or free operating cash.

Is under billing always a bad sign?

No. Under billing is normal when costs land just before a billing cycle closes, or when an approved change order has not been invoiced yet. It becomes a warning when the same job stays under billed for two or three months running, or when the balance grows while the billing excuse stays the same. Persistent under billing is often cost overrun that nobody has pushed into the estimate yet, which is how profit fade starts.

How do I calculate percent complete on a WIP schedule?

Divide cost incurred to date by the current estimated total cost of the job. If you have spent 624,000 dollars against an estimated total cost of 960,000 dollars, the job is 65 percent complete. Multiply that percentage by the contract value to get earned revenue. The method only works if the estimated total cost is updated every month; a stale estimate makes every downstream number wrong.

What is profit fade and how do I catch it early?

Profit fade is the pattern where a job's estimated gross margin shrinks as the job progresses, so profit recognized in early months gets clawed back near completion. Catch it by tracking each job's estimated margin month over month on the WIP schedule, questioning any drop from the bid margin, and treating aging under billings as unrecognized cost overruns until someone proves otherwise.

The WIP schedule is where construction profit and construction cash finally agree to meet. Build it monthly, book the entries, ask the fade questions, and both of your clocks start telling the same time.
